entry.title: Re: Crystal Reports x11 How to run stored procedure, popu...
entry.content:
The problem you'll run into is CRD report files saves the connection info, other than the password, in the RPT file.

Selecting a new DB may cause issues without Verifying the DB first.

Also note, you must select values for the SP before logging in, CR needs to pass those values when connecting, you can't pass null's

A few things to try:

For each report try changing the Verify options:

Then close CR, open the report, sometimes logging on first and then open the report may help

The other test is to use ODBC, set The Server name but not the Database, they may help also, not sure how CR with respond to this.

To enable CRLogger, CR's database logging component do this:

To use
crlogger to trace Crystal Reports (per SAP Note 1603398), set Windows environment variables to enable logs and specify a directory. Create a C:\logging folder, add LOGGING_DIR=C:\logging, LOGGING_ENABLED_ASSERT=1, and LOGGING_ENABLED_RUNTIME=100 in System Properties, then restart Crystal Reports to generate crw32_*.log files. 
Steps to Enable crlogger Tracing:
  1. Create Log Directory: Create a folder named logging on your C: drive (C:\logging).
  2. Set Environment Variables:
    • Open Windows search, type "environment variables," and select "Edit the system environment variables".
    • Click "Environment Variables" and under "System Variables," add the following:
      • LOGGING_DIR = C:\logging
      • LOGGING_ENABLED_ASSERT = 1
      • LOGGING_ENABLED_RUNTIME = 100
  3. Restart Application: Restart Crystal Reports or your application for the changes to take effect.
  4. Run Report: Open and refresh your report. Files named crw32_[PID]_[Date]_runtime.log will appear in C:\logging. 
How to Disable Logging:
Remove the environment variables created above or rename the crlogger.dll file, ensuring you restart the application afterward. 
Locating crlogger.dll:
  • 32-bit: C:\Program Files (x86)\SAP BusinessObjects\Crystal Reports for .NET Framework 4.0\Common\SAP BusinessObjects Enterprise XI 4.0\win32_x86
  • 64-bit: C:\Program Files (x86)\SAP BusinessObjects\Crystal Reports for .NET Framework 4.0\Common\SAP BusinessObjects Enterprise XI 4.0\win32_x64

entry.title: Focus of this Learning Group and Best Practices for Using the Forum
entry.content:
In this Learning Group we want to connect learners in order to exchange ideas and to provide mutual support on learning tasks in the Supply Chain area. 
Hence, we only want to address questions related to the training content given on Learning.SAP.com and SAP Learning Hub. 

When you post a question or start a discussion, it is both helpful and speeds up things for all involved if you mention the course ID, course title, collection (if known), unit/lesson/exercise/step (as applicable) – along with your query. This makes it way easier for both other learners and moderators/dispatchers to understand, classify, reproduce, and forward the item to the respective experts, if necessary. 

Note that for very different reasons, we can’t cover any consulting issues, though. If your question is related to a concrete consulting problem, we'd recommend not losing valuable time and putting this question to our Consulting Communities instead: https://community.sap.com/topics/supply-chain-management 

Thank you for your understanding!

entry.title: The Day in the Life of a Data Steward – Meet Susan
entry.content:
When Joe, a quota-driven sales rep, opened his monthly dashboard, he felt a familiar knot in his stomach. His quota attainment looked wrong — revenue numbers didn’t match what he saw in the company’s customer relationship management (CRM) system; some closed orders seemed to vanish, and his commission estimate was off. He needed answers fast. Enter Susan, the Sales Data Steward. 

Susan’s phone pinged with Joe’s message: “Numbers don’t add up — can you help?” She smiled; this was exactly the kind of problem her role was designed to solve. Over coffee, she walked Joe through how she would restore trust in his sales metrics — and how he could rely on a single source of truth going forward. 

Accelerate data projects with data products 

Susan started with the first goal, to build a Sales Performance data product that everyone — sellers, managers, finance — can trust. She connected the primary sources: SAP S/4HANA sales orders, the CRM opportunity records Joe used daily, and the billing system where invoices were issued. SAP Business Data Cloud catalog harvested metadata from each source so Susan could see where definitions diverged. 

She profiled the datasets, spotting familiar issues: duplicate orders, inconsistent currencies, and missing booking dates. To fix this she defined clear business semantics — canonical definitions for “revenue,” “closed order,” and “booking date” — using the unified semantic layer in Business Data Cloud. She set quality rules (standardize currency, reconcile order-to-invoice, remove duplicates) and implemented transformations in SAP Datasphere, a component of Business Data Cloud, to apply fixes and compute KPIs like revenue recognition and quota attainment. 

Before publishing the derived Sales Performance data product, Susan documented every transformation and lineage step in the catalog. When Joe asked how they could trace any KPI back to a source row, she showed him the lineage diagram — from invoice record to summed revenue — so audits and investigations became straightforward instead of detective work. 

A unified, trusted view of your business 

Joe also needed a single view of his accounts. Sometimes an account in CRM had multiple IDs in S/4HANA or marketing systems; duplicates made it hard for him to track customer history. Susan prioritized a customer 360 derived product that linked the consolidated customer master with transactional data (orders, returns, interactions). 

Using SAP Master Data Governance, Susan checked out the data quality dashboards to ensure customer master was up to standards – cleansed and consolidated into golden records. In the Business Data Cloud semantic layer, she defined account hierarchies and segmentation, so Joe’s territory reports and account rollups would always use the same definitions.  In addition, to protect sales contacts and sensitive fields, Susan applied sensitivity labels and masking in the data catalog and enforced access policies for downstream tools. That meant Joe could see the data he needed for selling without exposing PII (Personally Identifiable Information) beyond what his role allowed. 

Ensuring data integrity and trust 

Joe’s commission complaint was the final piece. Compensation calculations and territory mappings had been a source of friction — organizational changes and inconsistent territory definitions across the CRM and human resource (HR) systems were creating mismatches. Susan treated territory and compensation as governed data products: she registered territory and compensation primary products (SAP SuccessFactors for rep assignments, CRM for territories, and the compensation system) and modeled territory and quota semantics in the semantic layer. 

She produced derived products that calculated compensation and applied quota rules, with explicit lineage and timestamped transformation steps. Role-based access and masking protected who could view compensation details. Retention and archival tags supported audit requirements, so if finance or internal auditors asked for the computation trail, Susan could provide it immediately. 

Delivering value and promoting self-service 

Once sales performance, customer 360, and compensation data products were published, Susan put them into the SAP Datasphere Data Marketplace with clear, plain-language descriptions, common queries, KPIs, and her contact details. She added usage examples and steward notes in the catalog, so business users like Joe could discover and use the data without guessing which dataset was trusted. 

Because access was governed and published via delta share, Joe’s analytics tools could connect directly and safely. Susan also continued to monitor the master data, with data quality scores (e.g., completeness, duplicates, currency consistency). When a quality alert fired, she had the ability to execute remediation flows with Master Data Governance. 

Watch a demo of ensuring trusted data products with SAP MDG and SAP BDC

Turn fragmented sources into trusted, accessible data  

A week later Joe refreshed his dashboard. The numbers aligned. Quota attainment matched expectations, commission calculations were transparent, and his account views reflected the single customer 360 view. He could see exactly which invoice contributed to his recognized revenue and who to contact if something looked off — Susan’s catalog entry and contact information were a click away. 

Susan didn’t just fix a dashboard — she changed how the sales organization accessed and trusted data. By registering primary data products, defining business semantics, applying quality rules, and publishing steward-curated data products with documented lineage and governed access, she turned fragmented sources into reliable, reusable data. Joe felt empowered with self-serve analytics while knowing there was stewardship behind the numbers. 

Conclusion 

If you’re starting as a Data Steward, Susan’s playbook maps directly to the operational checklist: prioritize high-value domains, leverage Master Data Governance for trusted master data,  use Business Data Cloud to harvest metadata, register and profile primary products, define semantics in the unified layer, enforce quality and masking, publish derived products to the marketplace, and monitor KPIs and usage. The result is faster time-to-value for business users, fewer shadow-analytics, and auditable, repeatable outcomes for the organization. 

Recommended Resources 

Blog Post: What is a Business Data Fabric? 

Blog Post: What are data products? 

Best Practice Article: The converged data imperative: How to feed AI, govern data, and secure the future 

Blog Post: Master data is a strategic, business-critical asset 

Hands-on: SAP Business Data Cloud basic trial

entry.title: AI driven Composite Creation in SAP Analytics Cloud using SAP AI Core
entry.content:
PROBLEM STATEMENT

SAC (SAP Analytics Cloud) is a powerful business intelligence tool used to do powerful visualizations and meaningful analysis of data.

This project is intended to address a set of pain-points in SAP Analytics Cloud as described below.

Firstly, although SAP Analytics Cloud comes loaded with many useful charts and widgets, there sometimes exists a gap between what the user envisions and what is supported out of the box in terms of widgets. To bridge this gap, SAC provides the option to upload custom widgets built using HTML, CSS & JavaScript. However, the average SAC customer will not have the coding expertise required to build the custom widget and, thus, ends up dependent on another party to develop it for them.

Secondly, given a particular model or dataset, they might not always know which kind of visualisation would best suit the data. Choosing a less suitable form of representation reduces the value that the custom widget brings and undermines the effectiveness of the whole story.

Finally, built-in SAC widgets have the same design and look despite which customer company uses it. Although there is support for additional CSS based modifications on the widgets, it is quite restricted and doesn't support a lot of visual changes. In cases where the customer wants the widget to reflect their own brand identity, they must fully rely on coding custom widgets which is time consuming and requires coding expertise from the customer. These custom widgets can be converted into composites , so they can be used in various stories with different types of data. The styling will be maintained across all the widgets.

To sum up, the core issues that this project addresses are:

  • Eliminate coding knowledge requirement
  • Reduce chances of incorrect chart selection
  • Lack of customer branding/identity in widgets

Users can describe their visualization and give the data set or ask the chatbot to suggest charts based on the data. The system responds with the complete widget code or intelligent widget choices according to the data given. It chooses the charts and widgets accordingly based on the dataset and user prompts, and generates the complete widget code with the design personalized to the brand that the user wants.

This whole process needs no manual coding and the system also provides ready to deploy json which can be used in SAC directly

Folder structure created:

widget-name
   Src
      Widget_styling.js
      Widget.js
Widget-name.json

 

Architecture

 

There are 4 main components in the project.

The widget builder studio, Backend, LLM server and widget server.

The user interacts with the widget builder studio, it has a chatbot interface along with a widget preview panel. The LLM generated widget code can first be previewed and refined before deployment. If the code has syntax errors, the builder studio has an auto-fix layer which retries 3 times before it fails.

The backend serves as the orchestration layer between the user-facing chat interface and the AI-powered generation pipeline. Built on Express.js, it handles request routing, context assembly, and response formatting while maintaining a clean separation of concerns. It also handles file creation, parsing the LLM output, identifying the request type and routing the request to the LLM server with specific prompt. Uses prompt engineering to strictly enforce code structure while code generation.

The LLM server is enhanced by the RAG pipeline which consists of custom widget api documentation, widget syntax, widget examples and best practices. It also fetches brand details from brandFetch api and injects the information into the prompt. File server is used to serve the generated js files directly to SAC so that the user doesn’t have to manually upload the folder.

Backend

The backend maintains carefully crafted system prompts that act as guardrails for the LLM. These prompts enforce critical requirements: proper Web Component lifecycle methods and the specific section markers (===WIDGET_CONFIG===, ===MAIN_COMPONENT===, ===STYLING_PANEL===) that enable reliable parsing of generated code. Without this structured output format, extracting usable widget files from free-form LLM responses would be error-prone.

Backend Architecture

The backend serves as the orchestration layer between the user-facing chat interface and the AI-powered generation pipeline. Built on Express.js, it handles request routing, context assembly, and response formatting while maintaining a clean separation of concerns.

Request Flow

When a user submits a widget description, the backend receives the natural language prompt along with any uploaded data model context. Rather than forwarding raw requests directly to the LLM, the backend enriches them with structured system instructions that guide the AI toward generating SAC-compliant output. These instructions include widget structure templates, naming conventions, and data binding patterns derived from SAP's official documentation.

System Prompt Engineering

The backend maintains carefully crafted system prompts that act as guardrails for the LLM. These prompts enforce critical requirements: proper Web Component lifecycle methods and the specific section markers (===WIDGET_CONFIG===, ===MAIN_COMPONENT===, ===STYLING_PANEL===) that enable reliable parsing of generated code. Without this structured output format, extracting usable widget files from free-form LLM responses would be error-prone.

Data Model Context Injection

When users upload CSV files to describe their data structure, the backend parses column headers and infers dimensions versus measures. This context is injected into the prompt, enabling the LLM to generate appropriate data bindings and access

 

RAG pipeline

Rag pipeline is created to assist the LLM for extracting the right context and documentation information of custom widget that is needed to generate code for the required (js and json) files. The custom widget's documentation pdf is extracted as text page by page producing a list of document objects with metadata (page number, source filename). Each page gets doc_type as api_docs in its metadata so the RAG has better retrieval context.

To make sure the LLM has more context of the syntax and semantics to generate the widget code, supplementary markdown files are added which contain the full format, syntax to generate the code in. It also contains some custom widgets that are syntactically correct and follow the best practices.

The syntax reference markdown file is tagged doc_type: "syntax_reference". This contains the exact JSON schema, JS patterns, banned syntax rules , data binding structure, etc.

The vector database is also ingested with generated widget examples which follow the best practices. Doc_type is set to widget_example as metadata. This helps the LLM to prevent any sort of syntax errors and also follow the best practices.

Chunking Strategy

The chunker now routes documents to three different strategies based on their file_type metadata:

  • The api documentation pdfs are divided into chunks of 1000 characters with overlap of 200 characters so that no context is lost at chunk boundaries. The chunk is split at natural boundaries like new line, new page, etc
  • The js files are chunked not based on \n or paragraph split but based on function boundaries and the context of the file in the scope of the widget
  • The json files are kept as they are as separate chunks as they are

These chunks are embedded into dense vectors and stored in a FAISS index on disk for fast similarity search. The embedding model is cached to avoid reloading.

When the user enters a prompt, the system detects any brand names using the LLM itself (SAP AI core). Each brand name is used to make an api call to brand fetch and extract useful brand information needed to enhance the widget css and layout design.

Brand Data Extracted

  • Logo URLs
  • Icon URLs (smaller square logos)
  • Brand colors (hex values)
  • Brand information and about (helps in choosing the type of visualization)

FAISS performs the nearest neighbor search returning the top - k (15) most semantically similar document chunks from the database.

  • For Q/A queries → filter: doc_type = ["api_docs", "syntax_reference"]
  • For widget generation → filter: doc_types = ["widget_example", "syntax_reference"]

Using these documents are first filtered and 15 similar documents are extracted from FAISS similarity search.

These 15 documents are sent to the SAP AI core with compact summaries. The LLM is prompted as a relevance ranker, it compares the query and docs, reranks the 15 documents based on the information that is most important.

Why 15 → 5 instead of just fetching 5?

FAISS's embedding similarity is a coarse approximation. A document with the right keywords might rank #12 by cosine distance but be the most relevant result contextually. The LLM reranker can reason about content, not just vector proximity.

Example documents in the vector base:

[Document 1]
(type = widget_example, Source: coca-cola-brand-matrix.json, Page: N/A)

[Document 2]
(type = syntax_reference, Source: widget_syntax_reference.md, Page: N/A)

The prompt is assembled with system prompt, retrieved document context, brand context and the users original question. For widget generation, a special widget generation prompt is added which enforces strict outputting format with section markers so the preview doesn’t fail with bad syntax.

Validation & Error Handling

The code structure for the json is validated based on basic rules over what it has to contain. The js files are also validated on code structure and syntax errors before generation.

If any critical errors are found, the validation errors are formatted into a bullet list and sent to the LLM with the prompt:

"The following widget code has validation errors: [...]. Fix ONLY the errors listed. Output the COMPLETE fixed widget."

This acts as an internal error and syntax corrector before the widget is created. If there are errors after this process, the backend has retry loop where the LLM tries to correct the error logs with max retries set as thrice.

We observe almost nil percent of errors that still persist after the rag pipeline.

How to use it in SAC?

Once the widget is ready and satisfactory to the user, they can click on deploy widget and download the resulting json.

The json should be uploaded to the custom widgets section in SAC.

After creating the custom widget, it can be added in the story. The data can be selected and the measures and dimensions should be selected based on the given hints in SAC itself.

Example Outputs:

Intelligent chart selections provided by widget assistant

Widget preview

 

STORIES CREATED USING THIS FEATURE IN SAC:

Conclusion

We believe this tool can help business users and developers to create seamless dashboards for various applications. Stakeholders can design the dashboard using their own branding without spending a lot of time on coding custom widgets. This tool will also help stakeholders with no coding knowledge, generating extremely helpful dashboards to analyze available data.

Future work would be to integrate this tool into joule so that it can be used inside SAC directly.

Authors

@Praneel04@Dhakkshin

entry.title: Re: Error When Reverse GI. Stock Stuck in Blocked Stock I...
entry.content:
Hi,

if you are using scope item 5MQ the goods issue has 2 parts. You’ve already created the sales order and delivery and posted PGI. In first step stock moved to Valuated Stock in Transit. To “finish” the goods issue process in scope item 5MQ, you complete the External Transfer of Control step so the system clears VSIT and recognizes COGS. In Public Cloud this is typically done by recording Proof of Delivery (POD).

Please check your configuration in setup guide of scope item 5MQ.

entry.title: Re: Different consolidation group in handling unit Outbou...
entry.content: This error message “different consolidation group in handling unit” comes directly from SAP’s packing logic in S/4HANA. It means that the two deliveries you are trying to pack have different values in the consolidation group field, which is used by the system to decide whether deliveries can be combined into the same HU. Having said that, the consolidation group is determined by delivery header fields like shipping point, route, and transportation group. To pack two deliveries into one HU, those values must match. If they don’t, the workaround is to use nested HUs.

entry.title: Why sustainability is both a responsibility and an opportunity for a Hospital ?
entry.content:
For hospitals, sustainability is a core duty - patient care and sustainability must go hand in hand, because healing that harms is self-defeating.

Hospitals are places of healing. They exist to protect life, restore health, and support people during their most vulnerable moments. But what if the systems designed to care for us are quietly harming the very conditions that make health possible in the first place?
Consider this. The electricity used by a single hospital bed over the course of one year is roughly equivalent to the annual energy consumption of four row houses. Or take anesthesia. Just one hour using Desflurane can generate emissions comparable to driving 375 to 750 kilometers by car. These examples make one thing clear. Hospitals are essential to our wellbeing, and they also have a significant environmental footprint

Globally, the healthcare sector accounts for around 4.4 percent of total CO₂ emissions – if Healthcare would be a country it would be the 6th biggest emitter and more than double of international shipping. Hospitals contribute a large share of this impact due to their continuous operation and complex infrastructure. Heating and cooling systems run around the clock. Medical devices, pharmaceuticals, catering services, buildings and logistics all require large amounts of energy and resources. Water use alone is striking. In Germany, every single hospital bed consumes more than 200 thousand liters of water per year, which equals around 500 liters per day. Modern healthcare saves lives, and it is also highly resource intensive.

At the same time, environmental degradation directly affects human health. Climate change, air pollution and water scarcity increase the prevalence of allergies and infections and place additional strain on already burdened healthcare systems. Hospitals therefore face a dual responsibility. Caring for patients today and protecting the environmental conditions that make health possible tomorrow.

From insights to actions: how hospitals shape a sustainable future

One of the biggest challenges are insights. Many hospitals understand that sustainability matters but struggle to realize where their environmental impact actually arises. Emissions and resource usage are spread across everyday operations such as energy use, medical products, supply chains, infrastructure, waste, and catering. Without transparency, sustainability efforts remain fragmented, focus areas follow gut-feelings and opportunities for meaningful improvement are easily missed.

Sustainability becomes powerful when it is part of everyday decisions. Process- and data-driven insights help hospitals understand how care delivery, resource use, and responsibility are intertwined.

They make environmental impact visible across daily operations. This helps hospitals distinguish between what cannot be changed and where smarter use of resources is possible. A hospital bed must be cleaned after every patient, and hygiene standards are non-negotiable. What can change is how water is used across the hospital as a whole once consumption patterns and inefficiencies become visible.

Sustainability becomes a question of understanding and action rather than restriction.

  • Sustainability supports economic responsibility. When medical products are purchased, hospitals can understand how price, availability, and supply chains relate to one another. This creates transparency about whether a product’s environmental impact is in proportion to its economic value and clinical necessity. Sustainability becomes part of sound purchasing decisions rather than a trade off against efficiency.
  • Social responsibility is another essential part of this picture. Hospitals must function at all times, even during disruptions, crisis, or power outages. In operating rooms or intensive care units, power loss is not an option. Decisions about backup systems, energy independence and emergency preparedness directly affect patient safety. Digital insights help hospitals understand critical dependencies, assess risks and plan for resilience. This supports safer working conditions for staff and greater security for patients who depend on uninterrupted care.

 

Sustainability must become accessible to everyone and pa rt of everyday decisions. Reports and insights available without complex analyses  keep teams informed and enable decisions – AI powered -  based on reliable and current data.

Sustainability in healthcare is not about perfection. It is about awareness, responsibility and progress. It is about protecting health today and preserving the conditions for health tomorrow – act now.

Sustainability is what remains when we leave - Let us make sure healthcare leaves something that truly lasts.
Visit us on Sustainability Software, Solutions & Services | SAP and/or reach out to us or your SAP account manager to find out how SAP can support you in fulfilling your sustainability ambitions and provide exceptional care for your patients.

entry.title: Rehire and Onboarding (2.0)
entry.content:
Hello,  We are almost ready to implement Onboarding 2.0.  For moving temporary employees to regular employees our current state is that we future date their term and then process their onboarding/rehire with old employment.  With converting to Onboarding 2.0 we are informed that it does not support rehire with old employment AND allow to future date term and start onboarding for that person.  we would have to wait until they are actually termed and then initiate onboarding and have them complete all paperwork on their first day.  This will not work for us.

We are considering for those scenarios ONLY to use rehire with new employment but it would have downstream impact to IT/Active Directory.

I'm curious what others are doing that use both EC and Onboarding AND rehire with old employment.  How are you processing rehires with the new Onboarding?

entry.title: Creating Electronic Documents In Contract Accounting and Convergent Invoicing
entry.content:
This blog post is for developers and consultants, introducing the procedure how to create electronic documents (eDocuments) in Contract Accounting and Convergent Invoicing that are extended from the Financial Accounting and Sales and Distribution modules in SAP S/4HANA. Since it is an extension from the FI/SD modules, the customizing and processes of eDocuments for specific countries should already exist in the system.

Prerequisites

Customizing set up by Financial Accounting
(No action required for the users using Contract Accounting)

  1. Define the process steps in the eDocument Process (transaction: EDOP).
  2. Make sure that the FI/SD localization is available for creating electronic documents in Contract Accounting and Invoicing.

SAP Note Implementation for Contract Accounting or Convergent Invoicing

  1. For creating contract accounting electronic documents, implement the following SAP Notes:
    • 3342537
    • 3331737 (For non-Peppol countries)
    • 3381485 (For Peppol countries)
  2. For creating electronic documents in Convergent invoicing, activate event 2648 in the transaction FQEVENTS and assign FM FKC_EVENT_2648.
  3. For both Contract Accounting and Invoicing, you have set up the value mapping based on your business requirements.
    • First create your value mapping in the transaction /n/AIF/CUST.
    • Then fill with values in the value mapping in the transaction /N/AIF/VMAP.

How to Extend eDocument Creation Function from the FI/SD module?

Change the Method of Class

In the class builder transaction SE24, enter the class based on your application component as the Object Type, and change the implementation of CREATE_EDOC_INSTANCES method.
Note: For Contract Accounting, use the CL_EDOC_FACTORY_FICA_DOC class; for Convergent Invoicing, use the CL_EDOC_FACTORY_CI_DOCUM class.

Create your logic for eDocument create, you can find the example as follows:

  • You can create your own logic by creating a custom method with the prefix "Z" on line 12 and line 16 as follows. By doing so, you have your own customized method based on the SAP delivered method.

    You can find how to create a new class in the following setting.

  • Alternatively, you can also develop your own factory class. Meanwhile you must make sure that you add the factory class is maintained in the view EDOFACTORYV in the transaction SM30.

Determine eDocument Type for Contract Accounting Documents

Maintain the TFK003EDOC2 table. Determine eDocument type for contract accounting document with the accounting document type as shown in the following screen:

Assign eDocument Type to Convergent Invoice Document Type

For the Convergent Invoicing, use the view V_TFK26EDOC in SM30. You can determine the invoice types (invoices, credit memo, or debit memo) for the process:

Create a New Class for eDocuments

To create a new class that inherited from SD/FI module, follow the following instructions:

  1. Open the class builder transaction (SE24). Name your class and click "Create".
    See the example for Mexico eDocument process.
  2. Create a CONSTRUCTOR parameter with the following parameters:

    Example of the constructor logic for country Mexico is as follows:

Redefine your Class based on the Business Requirements

  1. For redefining classes, you must implement the following two methods with different purposes:
    • IS_RELEVANT decides if the contract accounting (FI-CA) document is relevant for the eDocument creation
    • DETERMINE_EDOC_TYPE decides what kind of eDocument type process is triggered
  2. In your newly created eDocument class with prefix "Z", insert class CL_EDOC_PROCESS in the  'Friends' section as shown in the following example.

    After the above settings you are able to create eDocuments and view them in the  EDOC_COCKPIT transaction.

AIF mapping

  1. Open the Class Builder transaction (SE24) create a mapping class with a prefix "Z".
    For example ZCL_EDOC_MAP_MX_FICA), which is inherited from the FI mapping CL_EDOC_MAP_MX.
  2. Create static public method GET_SOURCE_DOCUMENT_DATA_FICA. This method is called from the interface connector to move data from eDocument to AIF mapping.
    See the example of data transfer logic in Mexico solution below:
  3. Redefine methods you need to adjust for contract accounting (FI-CA) fields.
  4. Go to the /N/AIF/CUST transaction.
    Select the Define Interface activity and search for the interface created for the SD/FI solution of the specific country. For example, /EDOMX is the SD/FI solution for Mexico.
    Select the mapping structure name EDOC_’XX’_MAPPING_SOURCE that enables the extension of contract accounting fields.
  5. Open the Business Add-in (BAdI) transaction (SE18) and enter EDOC_INTERFACE_CONNECTOR in the BAdI name fields to create a new implementation.
  6. Use the following filter criteria in the implementation.
    • COUNTRY = ‘<COUNTRY_CODE>’
    • GENERIC_FILTER = ‘ZCL_EDOCUMENT_<COUNTRY_CODE>_CA’
    • INTERFACE_TYPE = ‘STANDARD’

Note: This generic filter is also used for creating the CONSTRUCTOR parameter in the previous paragraph. (See the orange arrow in the following picture.)

Create a new Badi implementation class, example ZCL_EDOC_INTF_CONN_”XX”_FICA inheriting from CL_EDOC_INTF_CONN_”XX” from the FI/SD module.
Redefine the method GET_SOURCE_DATA to call the mapping class and move the data from eDocument (this refers to point 2). See the example of Mexico below:

Additional Customizing Activities

The following customizing activities might differ for various document creations. For Contract Accounting enter the source type FICA_DOC. For Convergent Invoicing enter the source type  CI_DOCUM.

  1. Determine the interface ID for the process step and source type with the view EDOPROCSPINTDETV in the transaction SM30.
    Sample values for Mexico:
  2. Use the view EDOPROCSTEPDETV to determine the process step for each Interface ID and source type.
  3. Use the view EDOINTAIFV to assign the AIF interface to the corresponding Interface ID.
  4. Use the view EDOSOASERV to enter the eDocument SOA Service Ports.
  5. Use the view EDOINTV to assign a SOA Service to each interface ID.
  6. Use the view EDOMAPCLASSDETV to assign a mapping class to each process, the Interface ID, and the source type.
  7. Activate the company code for specific source type using the view EDOCOMPANYACTIV for the eDocument creation.
    • For Contract Accounting, use FICA_DOC
    • For Convergent Invoicing, use CI_DOCUM

Is this blog post useful to you? Leave your comments and let us know about your thoughts!

entry.title: Re: Why Data Readiness Is the Biggest Success Factor in SAP S/4HANA Migrations?
entry.content: Tks for this post.  A key point that is often underestimated is that data readiness is not only important for migration, but also for any kind of analytical or predictive use case after go-live. In procurement and supply chain scenarios, inconsistent master data or fragmented transactional history not only delay migration, but also limit the ability to identify patterns such as supplier performance or demand fluctuations. A common pitfall is treating data readiness as a one-time project activity. In practice, data quality needs to be continuously monitored and aligned with how data is actually created in daily operations. From an operational perspective, it is often more effective to start with a few critical data domains, such as vendor and material master data, and ensure they are consistent before expanding to broader datasets. This creates a stable foundation not only for S/4HANA processes, but also for any future analytics or AI-driven decision support.

entry.title: Questions About Display Options in the New SAP SuccessFactors Performance Management Form (New UI)
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

we are currently working with the new SAP SuccessFactors Performance Management form (latest User Interface) and we have a few questions regarding UI display options. I would appreciate your help or confirmation on whether the following items are configurable:

1. Can the Goal/Target Category be displayed in view mode?

When editing a Performance Target, the “Category” dropdown is visible.
However, when simply viewing the target (read‑only mode), the category is not shown.
Is there a way to display the category in view mode in the new UI?

 

2. Can the icons on the goal sections be removed?

In the new form UI, section icons (such as the circular “info” symbol next to Position Goals or Corporate Targets) appear by default.
Is there any option to hide or remove these icons?

3. Can the stars and the strikethrough symbol in the Overall Form Rating be removed?

In the Summary section, the Overall Form Rating displays star icons as well as a grey strikethrough symbol.
Is it possible to hide or remove these rating symbols in the new Performance Management UI, or are they system‑controlled?

Many thanks in advance for your help.

Best Regards,
Johannes.

entry.title: Re: DG Exceptions in ECC
entry.content: It is no longer working as when we create Safety Data Sheets, the exceptions are correctly showing in the DG master and the SDS itself. However, once sent by SDS Distribution, the customer is getting something completely different. They then notify us and we send the correct document. As there is no historical knowledge in the business currently on how this was originally set up and customised, we have failed at troubleshooting and I have been advising to create one specification for the bulk material with the correct DG. And a separate specification for the packaged materials with the exemptions. Therefore the system cannot get confused where material assignments are mixed up with other data and assigned to multiple sources. Some of our products fall under UN1866 PG III. However, when the material is < 25kg and is neither Skin Corrosive nor Acutely Toxic, we can apply the exemption under ADR/ADN/RID (Viscous Liquids 2.2.3.1.5).

entry.title: quantity-dependent component scaling - BOM
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition, is there any plan to support quantity-dependent component scaling directly within a single BOM item (for example, 2 components up to 400 pieces, 4 components from 800 pieces, etc.)?

Currently, it seems that the only way to model such scaling is to create multiple alternative BOMs and link them through separate production versions with defined lot-size ranges.

Will future releases allow quantity scaling at the BOM-item level, or will the approach via separate production versions remain the standard way to handle this requirement?

 

Kind regards,

Jana

entry.title: Re: Badi MD_EXT_SUP is not triggered in MD01n - MRP Live
entry.content:
Thanks for your blog.

We had a similar requirement as well, where we had to pick the issuing storage location from customizing and update in PR (EBAN-RESLO) in the BADI method : PPH_MRP_RUN_BADI->PURREQ_BEFORE_UPDATE_ADJUST.

In our case, the table field T460A-ADDIN contains the storage location, that we wanted to store in the PR -> Issuing storage location.

For example : In the case MRP run for Plant M004, with key M5, the BADI should set 1000 (from T460A-ADDIN last 4 characters)

Below is the SQL Script code that made it work for us. ✅

  METHOD if_pph_mrp_run_badi~purreq_before_update_adjust
         BY DATABASE PROCEDURE FOR HDB LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
         OPTIONS READ-ONLY USING t460a.

*{CHG0069051 - MRP Live - Badi to fill Issuing Storage location

-------------------------------------------------------------------
-- 1) Join IT_CTRL WITH T460A to fetch ADDIN and split it to get storage location
-------------------------------------------------------------------
    lt_reslo = SELECT DISTINCT
                        c.werks as werks,
                        c.sobsl as sobsl,
                        substring(a.addin, 5, 4)  AS reslo
                    FROM :it_ctrl AS c
                    INNER JOIN t460a AS a
                       ON a.werks = c.werks
                      and a.sobsl = c.sobsl;

--------------------------------------------------------------------
-- 2. UPDATE only reslo in lt_eban_new
--------------------------------------------------------------------
    lt_eban_new = SELECT the_index, doc_key_tmp, bnfpo, bsart, bstyp, bsakz, loekz, statu, estkz, frgkz, frgzu, frgst, ekgrp, ernam,
                        erdat, afnam, lastchangedatetime, creationdate, creationtime, txz01, matnr, ematn,
                        eban.werks,   --Plant
                        lgort, bednr, matkl, reswk, menge, meins, bumng, badat, lpein, lfdat, frgdt, webaz, preis, peinh, pstyp, knttp,
                        kzvbr, kflag, vrtkz, twrkz, wepos, weunb, repos, lifnr, flief, ekorg, vrtyp, konnr, ktpnr, infnr, zugba, qunum,
                        qupos, dispo, sernr, bvdat, batol, bvdrk, ebeln, ebelp, bedat, bsmng, lblni, bwtar, xoblr, ebakz, rsnum, sobkz,
                        arsnr, arsps, fixkz, bmein, revlv, vorab, packno,kanba, bpueb, cuobj, frggr, frgrl, aktnr, charg, umsok, verid,
                        fipos, fistl, geber, kzkfg, satnr, mng02, dat01, attyp, adrnr, adrn2, kunnr, emlif, lblkz, kzbws, waers, idnlf,
                        gsfrg, mprof, kzfme, spras, techs, mfrpn, mfrnr, emnfr, fordn, fordp, plifz, berid, uzeit, fkber, grant_nbr, memory,
                        banpr, rlwrt, blckd, revno, blckt, beswk, eprofile, eprefdoc, eprefitm, gmmng, wrtkz,
                        --Issuing Storage Location
                        CASE
                           WHEN f.werks IS NOT NULL THEN f.reslo
                            ELSE eban.reslo
                        END AS reslo,
                        --Issuing Storage Location
                            kblnr, kblpos, prio_urg, prio_req, memorytype, anzsn, mhdrz, iprkz, nodisp, srm_contract_id, srm_contract_itm,
                            budget_pd, eldat, iuid_relevant, sgt_scat, sgt_rcat, fsh_season_year, fsh_season, fsh_collection, fsh_theme
                       from :ct_eban_new as eban
                       left outer join :lt_reslo as f
                         on f.werks = eban.werks;

--------------------------------------------------------------------
-- 3. RETURN updated table
--------------------------------------------------------------------
    ct_eban_new = SELECT eban.* from :lt_eban_new as eban;

  endmethod.

In your case, you may want to derive the storage location from other source, so you can change the step-1 of the above code as per your customizing and use it in step-2.

To be honest, this was lot more simpler and quicker to achieve in ABAP and I really had to scratch my head whole day to make it work. In step 2, I had to list all the fields of CT_EBAN_NEW in the SELECT statement, even if I had to change only 1 field.

It is really annoying to deal with such a SQL script for just 1 field update, but there is no other choice, as the AMDP BADI is executed in HANA DB level, so ABAP code can't be used here whatsoever. 

Hope this will help.

Regards,

Bharat Bajaj

entry.title: SAP AI Core: Authentication Errors and How to Fix Them (Part 1 of 2)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  TROUBLESHOOTING  |  SAP AI CORE  |  PART 1 OF 2

SAP AI Core: Authentication Errors and How to Fix Them

Every error between your first curl command and a working bearer token — what causes them, what they actually mean, and the exact fix for each one. Part 1 of 2.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  SAP AI Core Troubleshooting Series  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Getting your first SAP AI Core bearer token working on Windows is harder than it should be. This article documents every authentication error between zero and a working token — PowerShell curl alias conflicts, quote character issues, port errors, missing OAuth path, empty responses, and RBAC denials — with the exact cause and fix for each. Part 2 covers deployment and configuration errors once authentication is working.

Why This Article Exists

The SAP AI Core documentation covers the happy path well. What it doesn't cover is the sequence of errors most practitioners hit on Windows before they ever get a working API call. These errors are not obscure — they come up consistently across different BTP setups, different teams, and different experience levels.

This article documents six authentication errors in the order they typically appear, based on real troubleshooting sessions. Each error has a clear cause and a specific fix. No generic advice — only what actually works.

Part 2 (Friday) covers what happens after authentication works: 404 on deployment calls, two-instance confusion, resource group provisioning failures, and orchestration vs foundation-models mismatches.

BEFORE YOU START 

What You Need

This guide assumes you have an SAP AI Core service instance on BTP with at least one service key created. From the service key JSON you need four values:

  • url — the OAuth authentication base URL (e.g. https://your-subdomain.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com)
  • clientid — your OAuth client ID
  • clientsecret — your OAuth client secret
  • serviceurls.AI_API_URL — your AI Core API base URL

The url field in your service key does NOT include /oauth/token. You must append it manually. This is the single most common cause of authentication failures and is covered in Error 5 below.

ERROR 1 OF 6  
  ERROR 1    PowerShell curl alias — -X not recognized    ParameterBindingException 
What You See
Invoke-WebRequest : A parameter cannot be found that matches parameter name 'X'.
At line:1 char:6
+ curl -X POST 'https://...authentication.../oauth/token'
What Causes It

PowerShell has a built-in alias called curl that actually runs Invoke-WebRequest — Microsoft's own HTTP cmdlet. Invoke-WebRequest uses completely different syntax and does not understand the -X flag that real curl uses. When you type curl in PowerShell you are not running curl.exe — you are running Invoke-WebRequest.

The Fix

Two options — use Command Prompt instead of PowerShell, or explicitly call curl.exe to bypass the alias:

# Option 1 — Open Command Prompt (cmd) instead of PowerShell
# All curl commands in this guide use double quotes — required in cmd
 
# Option 2 — Stay in PowerShell but force the real curl binary
curl.exe -X POST "https://your-subdomain.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" ^
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=YOUR_ID&client_secret=YOUR_SECRET"

All remaining examples in this guide use Command Prompt syntax with double quotes. If you prefer PowerShell, add .exe after curl and the commands will work identically.

ERROR 2 OF 6  
  ERROR 2    Curly quotes / bad characters    curl: (3) URL rejected: Port number was not a decimal number between 0 and 65535 
What You See
curl: (3) URL rejected: Port number was not a decimal number between 0 and 65535
What Causes It

When you copy a curl command from a web page, Teams message, email, or PDF, the quote characters are often converted to typographic curly quotes (“ ”) instead of straight ASCII quotes (" "). Curly quotes look identical on screen but are invisible corruption to curl — it cannot parse the URL correctly and reports a nonsensical port error.

This error is almost always a quote character issue. The URL itself is correct.

The Fix

Do not paste the URL from a rich text source. Type the URL manually, or paste into a plain text editor (Notepad, VS Code) first to strip formatting, then copy from there into the terminal.

Also ensure all quote marks in your command are straight ASCII double quotes (") — not curly (“”) or single (‘’). In Command Prompt, single quotes are not valid string delimiters at all.

# Wrong — curly quotes from copy-paste (looks fine, breaks silently)
curl -X POST “https://...authentication.../oauth/token”
 
# Correct — straight ASCII double quotes
curl -X POST "https://...authentication.../oauth/token"
ERROR 3 OF 6  
  ERROR 3    Single quotes in Command Prompt    curl: (3) URL rejected 
What You See
curl: (3) URL rejected: Port number was not a decimal number between 0 and 65535
What Causes It

Single quotes work as string delimiters in PowerShell and Linux/Mac terminals. They do not work in Windows Command Prompt. If you copy a curl command that uses single quotes (common in documentation written for Linux) and run it in cmd, Command Prompt interprets the single quote as part of the URL string — causing the same port parsing error as curly quotes.

The Fix

Replace all single quotes with double quotes when running curl in Command Prompt:

# Wrong — single quotes (Linux/Mac syntax, fails in Windows cmd)
curl -X POST 'https://...authentication.../oauth/token' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded'
 
# Correct — double quotes (Windows cmd syntax)
curl -X POST "https://...authentication.../oauth/token" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded"

In Command Prompt, line continuation uses ^ (caret) not \ (backslash). If you copy Linux curl commands with \ at the end of each line, remove them and replace with ^ or put the entire command on one line.

ERROR 4 OF 6  
  ERROR 4    Empty response — no output at all    Command exits with no output 
What You See

The curl command runs, the prompt returns, but nothing is printed. No error, no response, no JSON — just silence.

What Causes It

Three common causes in order of likelihood:

  • The -d parameter contains an & character that Command Prompt interprets as a command separator — splitting your command into two separate commands, one of which runs silently
  • A missing closing quote somewhere in the command causes cmd to wait for more input silently
  • The response is very large and the terminal is suppressing it — redirect to a file to capture it
The Fix

The & in client_credentials&client_id=... must stay inside double quotes. If it's outside quotes, Command Prompt treats it as a command separator:

# Wrong — & outside quotes splits the command
curl -X POST "https://.../oauth/token" -d grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=abc
 
# Correct — entire -d value inside double quotes
curl -X POST "https://.../oauth/token" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" ^
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=YOUR_ID&client_secret=YOUR_SECRET"

If you suspect a large response is being suppressed, redirect output to a file:

curl -X POST "https://.../oauth/token" ... > output.txt

Open output.txt — the full response will be there regardless of terminal display limitations.

ERROR 5 OF 6  
  ERROR 5    401 Unauthorized — missing /oauth/token path    {"error":"unauthorized"} 
What You See
{"error":"unauthorized","error_description":"An Authentication object was not found in the SecurityContext"}
What Causes It

The url field in your AI Core service key JSON is the base authentication domain — it does not include the /oauth/token path. Many practitioners use this URL directly and hit 401 because they're calling the base domain rather than the token endpoint.

Compare these two:

# What's in your service key url field (base domain — not the token endpoint)
https://your-subdomain.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com
 
# What you need to call (token endpoint — append /oauth/token manually)
https://your-subdomain.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token
The Fix

Always append /oauth/token to the url value from your service key. The SDK handles this automatically — but when calling directly with curl you must add it yourself:

curl -X POST "https://your-subdomain.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" ^
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=YOUR_CLIENTID&client_secret=YOUR_SECRET"

A successful token call returns a JSON object containing access_token — a long base64-encoded string. This is your bearer token. Copy everything between the quotes after "access_token": and use it in all subsequent AI Core API calls. Tokens expire after 3600 seconds (1 hour) — generate a fresh one for each session.

ERROR 6 OF 6  
  ERROR 6    RBAC access denied on resource group creation    {"error":{"code":"403"}} 
What You See
{"error":{"code":"403","message":"RBAC: access denied"}}
What Causes It

RBAC access denied on a resource group PUT call means your service key does not have admin scope for this AI Core instance. Service keys are created with specific role assignments — a key created without aicore_admin scope can generate tokens and list resources but cannot create or modify resource groups.

This is not a token expiry issue and not a URL issue. The token is working — the credentials behind it simply don't have the permission required.

The Fix

First — check if a resource group already exists before trying to create one. Many AI Core instances provisioned by BTP have a default resource group already:

curl -X GET "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/admin/resourceGroups" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN"

If a resource group already exists with status PROVISIONED — use it. There is no need to create a new one.

If you genuinely need to create a new resource group and are hitting RBAC denial, recreate the service key with admin permissions:

  1. BTP Cockpit → Services → Instances → your AI Core instance → Service Keys
  2. Delete the existing service key
  3. Create a new service key — confirm aicore_admin role is included in the parameters
  4. Generate a new token using the new service key credentials

Check existing resource groups before attempting to create new ones. Most RBAC errors on resource group creation are unnecessary — the default resource group already exists and has status PROVISIONED. The GET call above takes 10 seconds and saves significant debugging time.

QUICK REFERENCE 

Authentication Error Quick Reference

 

Error / Symptom

Root Cause

Fix

ParameterBindingException — -X not recognized

PowerShell curl alias intercepting command

Use cmd or call curl.exe explicitly

Port number not decimal between 0 and 65535

Curly quotes from copy-paste corrupting URL

Type URL manually or paste through plain text editor first

Same port error with single quotes

Single quotes invalid in Windows cmd

Replace all single quotes with double quotes

No output — command exits silently

& in -d value treated as command separator by cmd

Keep entire -d value inside double quotes

401 — Authentication object not found

Calling base auth URL without /oauth/token suffix

Append /oauth/token to url value from service key

403 — RBAC access denied

Service key lacks aicore_admin scope

Check existing resource groups first — recreate key if creation is genuinely needed

THE WORKING COMMAND 

Complete Working Token Call for Windows Command Prompt

Once all six errors are resolved, this is the complete working token call. All values in capitals are replaced with your service key values:

curl -X POST "https://YOUR_SUBDOMAIN.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" ^
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=YOUR_CLIENTID&client_secret=YOUR_SECRET"

A successful response looks like this — the access_token value is your bearer token:

{"access_token":"eyJhbGci...very long string...","token_type":"bearer","expires_in":3599}

Copy the full access_token value. In Part 2 we use it to call model deployments, diagnose 404 errors, resolve two-instance confusion, and fix resource group provisioning failures.

If your response contains access_token — authentication is complete. Every error in this article is behind you. Part 2 covers everything from this point to a working model response.

P.S. aDSO target is shared by other process chains which works fine, the only issue is within this process. To me it seems a bug of the process but i'd like to listen other opinions and possible solutions.

Thank you in advance!

entry.title: Re: Validation of document item text does not work with a...
entry.content:
Good day @djwafelap 

Thank you for the clarification.

Your current rule validates DocumentItemText in the Journal Entry business context. However, in Create Supplier Invoice, the user maintains SupplierInvoiceItemText on the editable supplier invoice items.

That means your current rule is validating the final accounting document item text, while your business requirement is about the editable supplier invoice item text which you enter in the app. So, based on your description, the reason why the behaviour is inconsistent is:

  • The user enters text in all editable invoice items
  • But the validation still fails because DocumentItemText is not the same thing as SupplierInvoiceItemText in this process.

So, based on your clarification, Create Supplier Invoice stores and exposes the editable text as SupplierInvoiceItemText, but your validation checks DocumentItemText in the journal entry context. So, even if every editable invoice item has text, the validation can still fail because it is not validating the same field the user is maintaining.

See the following references for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/8837a41d09744bf7963720c41c9f5b83.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/bb9f1469daf04bd894ab2167f8132a1a/913c5bcd8a434d7a824884e4a43966db.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/8837a41d09744bf7963720c41c9f5b83.html?locale=en-US

While your validation setup is not technically wrong, it is not aligned with the actual requirement. It is technically valid to check DocumentItemText but if your business requirement is only to enforce text addition for every editable invoice item in Create Supplier Invoice, then DocumentItemText is too broad and too late in the process. The reason is, it validates the accounting document outcome, not the user the item the user entered in the invoice field.

In contrast to what your rule is enforcing, your business requirement to actually ensure that every editable supplier invoice item must have SupplierInvoiceItemText. Those are not the same requirement in Create Supplier Invoice. This also explains why the same rule may work fine in Create Incoming Invoice and Post General Journal Entries, but not in Create Supplier Invoice. The reason is, supplier invoice app uses its own supplier invoice item model before the accounting document is derived.

As a recommendation, consider implementing your requirement in a way that validates the supplier invoice item text field that your users can maintain in the app. See KBA 3066300 for more insights.

You may also consider introducing an enhancement for supplier invoice rather than a journal entry validation.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Manager not updating in SuccessFactors Learning for one user
entry.content:
Hi @Shehan ,

Different issues might be causing this scenario... I think the first step is to check whether the data of user 36026791 is being correctly consumed within LMS.

I assume your company uses the User Connector - SF to create / update users in LMS. So, to check what LMS is consuming, please go to Learning Administration > System Administration > Connectors > User Connector - SF > click on 'Show archived input files' > for a file you assume the affected user's data should be present, click on 'Download'. At the downloaded file, search for the user 36026791 and check what information is preset at the column that represents the MANAGER data (the exact name depends on each customer... Can be MANAGER, or SUPERVISOR, etc.).

If the expected manager of 36026791 is present in the file, then an error might be happening for the user 36026791. This can be checked still at the User Connector - SF page, by clicking on 'View Results'. For the execution for which the input file was downloaded for (that is, you need to look at the results that represent the exact file you were checking), see if there are any exceptions. If yes, then click on the arrow pointing down from the 'View Details' column. A new window will load. Search if the user 36026791 is mentioned there. If yes, use the error message to troubleshoot the exception.

Best regards,

entry.title: Re: Creating 64bit System-DSN fals with errorcode 193 on...
entry.content:
The error -193 indicates that there is an architectural difference between the ODBC driver manager and the ODBC driver.  So, the ODBC Administrator is likely compiled as a ARM64 application so is expecting ARM based drivers. The "workaround" addresses the issue by adding the x64 driver and DSN details into the registry bypassing the ODBC Administrator.  When 64 bit was initially added, the registry (plus others) where separated into 32 bit (Wow64) and 64 bit hives. As long as the 64 bit entries can be resolved in the registry, the ODBC Administrator is not used (afaik). 

Perhaps dbdsn might provide an alternative way to maintain the DSN.

entry.title: Lock Message During Role Type Creation for customer projects under Professional Services in CBC
entry.content:
Hi Team,

We are currently performing Role Type creation for customer projects under Professional Services. During the save step, the system displays a dialog with the options “Continue with the lock” or “Cancel lock.”

Please note that this configuration is being carried out in SAP Central Business Configuration (CBC). Upon checking the system message, it indicates the following behavior:

  • Selecting “Continue with the lock” will confirm the existing locks and allow the configuration to be saved.
  • Selecting “Cancel lock” will close the dialog, and the configuration changes will not be saved.

In this scenario and whether it is safe to continue with the lock while saving the configuration as the lock will not be removed until the Transport imported into the Test environment. We need to edit the configuration further as the discussion with the business still in progress on this regard.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Lock Message During Role Type Creation for customer p...
entry.content:
Dear Customer,

I hope you are well. 

The system message indicates that Continue with the lock will save the configuration while keeping the existing lock active. This is expected behavior for transport-controlled business configuration. The option is safe from a technical save perspective, but it does not release the lock, and further changes to the same configuration object may remain restricted until the related transport is processed in the downstream system.

If the later completion belongs to the same locked configuration object as the Role Type currently being saved, you should not assume that it can still be edited before the current transport import. In that case, the follow-up adjustment would more likely need to be done in a subsequent Edit/change cycle after the lock is released (transport is completed).

3728569- Lock Message During Role Type Creation For Customer Projects Under Professional Services In Central Business Configuration https://me.sap.com/notes/3728569

Best regards, 

Tiago 
SAP Technical Support

Running command near end of upgrade?

Unfortunately, nothing is being written to the installData logs during this step, so we have nothing actionable for making the install go faster.

Does this situation happen to anyone else?!?

entry.title: Re: Week 3 : Use RAP Business Logic Prediction to Implement Validation
Looking for repeatable step/command to confirm connectivity to HSM within ASE
entry.content:
Once Sybase ASE instance configured for using an external key (HSM via PKCS) is restarted there is generally minimal interaction with the HSM (Appears once decryption of the keys take place then)

I would like to see if there are any steps, or a specific command that would confirm connectivity to the HSM (to ensure availability). Could you please advise?

In summary I would like to ifunderstand  there were any commands or functions that would safely confirm access to the HSM (ie a command that completes successfully, reports upon availability - or is guaranteed to cause using the HSM) without any destructive action.


--- Steps to Reproduce ---

SAP ASE configured to use an external keysore (HSM) via PKCS library

sp_configure 'external keystore'
go

sp_encryption 'hsm_credential',  'lib=libPortalPKCS11.so; pin=xxxxxxxx; slot=0'
go

and create the key with

create encryption key hsm_key on external keystore
go

create encryption key master
go

create encryption key dbenc_key for database encryption
go

Create a database and encrypt/decrypt etc

At this stage I would like to understand if there were any commands or functions that would safely confirm access to the HSM (ie a command that completes successfully, reports upon availability - or is guaranteed to cause using the HSM)


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Looking for repeatable step/command to confirm connec...
entry.content:
It appears that ASE does not provide a dedicated command to verify HSM connectivity. Instead, you can try creating a master key with the HSM key in a user database or a temp database (one that does not already have a master key). If the master key is created successfully and no related errors appear in the server error log, this confirms that the HSM is available and functioning

For example:

  •  master key can be created, then HSM is available:

1> create encryption key master with external key

go

2> 1> sp_encryption help

2> go

 Key Name                         Key Owner

       Key Length

       Key Algorithm

       Key Type                                 Pad

       Initialization Vector

       Protected By

       Key Recovery

       # of Key Copies

 -------------------------------- ----------------------------------------

       ------------------------------------------

       ------------------------------------------------

       ---------------------------------------- ----------------------------

       ----------------------------------------------------------------

       ----------------------------------------------

       ----------------------------------------------

       ----------------------------------------------------

 sybencrmasterkey                 dbo

                         256

       AES

       symmetric master key                                  0

                                      0

       hsm key

                             0

                                0

 

(1 row affected)

(return status = 0)

 

 

  •  master key can’t be created, then HSM is not available
  • 1> create encryption key master with external key
  • go
  • 2> 1> sp_encryption help
  • 2> go
  • Msg 19603, Level 16, State 1:
  • Procedure 'sp_encryption', Line 2090:
  • There are no encryption keys (key copies) like '%' in 'tempdb'.
  • (return status = 1)
  •  
  • Example server errorlog: 
  • 00:0002:00000:00000:2026/03/23 07:17:21.78 kernel  hsm__encrypt_key_n_salt: Could not found unique key on HSM device with UUID '5074faa41b134e48b58a8b86f97ac800'
